metric_value: $1 billion+ (68% increase over prior year)
notes: Total revenues for CIS software suppliers; 68% YoY growth

metric_name: Customer support/help desk share of CIS market
metric_value: Over 60% of CIS market
notes: Traditional call center support/help desk dominates CIS market in 1997

metric_value: $7.3 million
notes: VC funding at time of eService launch

metric_value: Transition from call tracking to problem resolution and service delivery
notes: Call tracking alone insufficient; sophisticated problem resolution + knowledgebases required

metric_value: Customer self-service gains prominence — bypassing call center, 24x7 access, reduced wait times
notes: Companies providing direct customer access to support application

metric_value: Integration with complementary enterprise-class applications (SFA, field service, manufacturing, logistics, billing)
notes: Support enhanced by access to other CIS applications and core operational systems

metric_value: Increasing use of multimedia — images, schematics, video, remote session control to enhance support interactions
notes: Traditional voice-only call center inadequate for complex problem diagnosis

metric_value: Thin-client; Netscape or IE browser; object-oriented ActiveX; call control server; firewall-secured; single central administration site
notes: Application logic in ActiveX; Java capabilities planned Q4 1997; real-time remote desktop control capability

metric_value: Built from ground up for customer (not support agent) as primary end-user; natural language problem description; personalized interface
notes: Traditional CIS designed for trained support agents, not customers; eService designed for self-service

metric_value: Not inherently designed for customer self-service; complex navigation; specialized training required; Web front-ends bolted on as afterthought
notes: Aberdeen documents design mismatch of incumbent CIS for self-service use case

metric_value: eService administered from single centralized site; latest version auto-downloaded at each session; eliminates client-side upgrade coordination
notes: Contrasted with client-server: portion of logic on every client, requiring coordinated upgrades across thousands of desktops

metric_value: Category creation: first-mover in pure internet-native CIS; distinct market segment vs. call tracking incumbents
notes: Silknet has 'no legacy baggage' — pure internet architecture advantage

metric_value: Must maintain client-server for installed base while developing internet solution; hybrid web front-end strategy inadequate
notes: One-size-fits-all web access at single price point shows vendors haven't come to terms with market shift

metric_value: Internet-based CIS self-service is only scratching the surface; virtual call centers, peer forums, universal access are the real value
notes: Aberdeen identifies customer-directed support forums as 'virtually free support mechanism'

metric_value: As with all fundamental technology shifts, some suppliers will make transition to Internet, others will not
notes: Aberdeen prescient on CIS vendor attrition during internet architecture shift

metric_name: Aberdeen assessment of 3 successful ISV design tenets
metric_value: Successful internet CIS ISVs must excel at: 1) Ease-of-Use, 2) Solution/Information Access, 3) Customer Communication
notes: Three-part framework for evaluating internet CIS vendors

metric_value: 68% revenue increase from 1995 to 1996; $1B+ total market
notes: Highest growth segment of enterprise software in 1996

metric_name: eService launch date and first-mover status
metric_value: Introduced March 10 1997; first viable internet-native CIS; caused 'desperate scramble among competitors to catch-up'
notes: Aberdeen uses 'first viable' qualifier; distinguishes from hybrid web front-end approaches

metric_value: Unless competitors move fast, leadership in this new internet CIS market is Silknet's for the taking
notes: Aberdeen's explicit market leadership prediction for Silknet

metric_value: As software suppliers address new user requirements, new market leaders will emerge; incumbent call-tracking vendors at risk
notes: Aberdeen predicts wholesale leadership shift in CIS

metric_value: Total CIS ISV market reaching $3.4 billion by year 2000
notes: Aberdeen market sizing forecast for CIS software

metric_value: Acquired by Kana Communications for $4.2B in Feb 2000; combined entity served ~450 enterprise clients including eBay
notes: Source: WSJ 2000-02-07 https://www.wsj.com/articles/SB94993139567693324; CNET https://www.cnet.com/tech/tech-industry/kana-buys-silknet-in-4-2b-stock-swap/

metric_name: CIS market leader disruption — actual outcome
metric_value: Salesforce.com (2000), RightNow Technologies (2000), Kana/Silknet established internet CIS dominance; traditional call-tracking vendors (Clarify, Vantive) absorbed or marginalized
notes: Aberdeen's prediction of incumbent disruption proved correct; Siebel/Oracle acquired CRM leaders; SaaS-native vendors won new deployments

metric_value: CIS/CRM market exceeded $3.4B estimate; Gartner reported CRM software market at ~$3.7B in 2000, exceeding Aberdeen's forecast
notes: CRM became larger category; Aberdeen's 1997 estimate was directionally correct
